topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notification, stark white on the screen, felt like a physical blow. Elias stared at the figures, his breath catching in his throat until it was a shallow, ragged thing. The scholarship. It was his lifeline, the only thing keeping the looming shadow of debt and failure at bay. He reread the fine print, the clauses, searching for a loophole, a misplaced comma that would somehow validate his claim. His hands, slick with sudden cold sweat, trembled as he crumpled the paper into a tight ball, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet dorm room. He paced the worn rug, the floorboards groaning under his frantic movements. Every corner of his vision seemed to narrow, focusing only on the glaring discrepancy between the promised award and the reality staring back at him. He had to call someone. He had to fix this, right now, before the silence swallowed him whole.

[story 2]

"No. Absolutely not." Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. She gripped the edge of the desk so tightly her knuckles were white monuments against the dark wood. The email had landed like a stone in still water, shattering the carefully constructed reality she had built around her future. She felt a strange, hollow ache bloom in her chest, a crushing weight that made it hard to draw a full breath. She kept wiping at her eyes, but the tears refused to come, trapped behind a wall of numb shock. Her gaze drifted to the acceptance letter tacked above her monitor—a symbol of everything she had sacrificed for. A tremor ran through her hands as she slowly reached for her phone, her fingers fumbling over the screen, needing to verify, needing to know if this was a cruel trick, a mistake, or simply the final, crushing end of her hope.

[story 3]

Mark leaned against the cold brick wall outside the library, the late afternoon sun doing little to warm the chill settling deep in his bones. He kept running his thumb over the worn leather of his backpack, a repetitive, anxious motion. The confirmation letter sat on the table, stark and unforgiving under the harsh fluorescent lights. It detailed the funding allocation, and the name listed at the top was not his. A sudden, sharp intake of air made his chest tighten painfully. He squeezed his eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the details—the exact figures, the official seals—replayed behind his eyelids, relentless. He took a stumbling step forward, his jaw clenched so hard he could taste metal. He needed an explanation, a reason, anything to anchor himself before the world tilted completely off its axis.
